location: src/main/orky/orky-action-dispatcher.ts:166 (doResolveEscalation fallbackArgs, '--decision') and :247-250 (doRecordHumanGate args, '--evidence') / src/shared/orky-action-validate.ts:26-35 (validateFeatureSlug), :47-59 (requireString/requireNonEmptyString) — REQ-008, REQ-010, REQ-011
claim: The dispatcher passes human-authored free text (`decision` on the gatekeeper resolve-escalation fallback path, `evidence` on recordHumanGate) directly as a raw, un-encoded CLI argument VALUE to Orky's own gatekeeper CLI. Read C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js's `parseArgs` (lines 30-41): it scans the ENTIRE argv array for any token starting with `--`, not merely the token immediately following a recognized flag name, and if the token FOLLOWING a `--flag` also starts with `--`, it sets that flag's value to boolean `true` (does NOT consume it) and then re-scans the following token itself as an independent new flag. Because `decision`/`evidence` are always the LAST element of their respective argv arrays (`['resolve-escalation','--feature',featureDir,'--id',escalationId,'--decision',decision]` and `['record','--feature',featureDir,'--gate',gate,'--verdict',verdict,'--evidence',evidence]`), any value beginning with `--` is reinterpreted as a CLI flag instead of being carried through as the literal text. Neither `orky-action-validate.ts`'s `validateResolveEscalationRequest`/`validateRecordHumanGateRequest` nor the dispatcher itself reject or sanitize a `decision`/`evidence` string starting with `--`. I reproduced this adversarially by copying `parseArgs` verbatim from `C:/dev/Orky/plugin/gatekeeper/cli.js` and feeding it the exact argv the dispatcher constructs (command run in this session, not against any real project): (1) `evidence:'--force'` for `recordHumanGate` with `gate:'human-review', verdict:'pass'` parses to `{ ..., evidence: true, force: true }` — i.e. the human's `evidence` text is silently DROPPED (replaced by boolean `true`) and the literal `--force` flag is injected into gatekeeper's own `record(feature, gate, verdict, evidence, force)` call (`cli.js:91`: `a.force === true || a.force === 'true'` evaluates to `true`), even though `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`'s own code/tests (TEST-242) assert 'the invocation never includes --force' — that assertion is about the literal ARRAY the dispatcher constructs, not about what the CLI's parser ultimately derives from a value inside that array, so it does not catch this. (2) `decision:'--anything is fine'` for the `resolveEscalation` gatekeeper fallback parses to `{ ..., decision: true, 'anything is fine': true }` — `gatekeeper.js:627` then executes `esc.decision = decision || ''` with `decision === true`, so `state.json`'s recorded `escalations[].decision` field is silently corrupted to the boolean `true` instead of the human's actual decision text. Impact: (a) DATA INTEGRITY — the human decision/evidence text this feature exists to durably and accurately record (the enterprise-arch concern, REQ-013's whole rationale) is silently lost/replaced for any input starting with `--`, which is not a contrived edge case (e.g. a reviewer typing '--- see PR #42 ---' or literally describing a CLI flag in their reasoning); (b) DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH DEGRADATION for REQ-008 — the spec explicitly requires the gate restriction to be enforced 'independent of (and in addition to) gatekeeper.js's own enforcement' specifically as defense-in-depth, and requires the invocation to 'NEVER pass --force'; this finding shows attacker/user-controlled `evidence` text CAN cause `--force` to reach `gatekeeper record` regardless of the dispatcher's own hard-coded args array. It is currently INERT only because `gate` is independently pre-validated to `{brainstorm,human-review}` before the CLI ever runs (so `record()`'s `!HUMAN_GATES.has(gate) && !force` bypass check is never reached in the false/true branch that matters) — i.e. safety today rests on ONE remaining correct layer, not the two independent layers REQ-008 says it wants; a future regression in the gate pre-check (the exact class of bug reviews exist to catch) would make this a live human-gate-restriction bypass, not merely a data-corruption bug. `feature`/`escalationId` are also passed as raw argv values (`--feature <slug>` in the feedback emit path, `--id <escalationId>` in the gatekeeper fallback) and are similarly reinterpretable if they start with `--`, though lower-likelihood in practice (`feature` must already resolve to an EXISTING `.orky/features/<slug>` directory before any CLI runs, and `--payload`'s JSON content was verified, by the same repro, to survive intact even when `feature` is corrupted — so this narrower sub-case mainly causes wrong feature-attribution in the emitted feedback event, not further injection). `title`/`detail`/`phase` (submitWork) are unaffected because they are always carried inside the JSON-encoded `--payload` argument, never as raw argv.
fix: Before constructing any argv array passed to `runOrkyCli`, reject (return `errorKind:'invalid-args'`, CONV-001-actionable) any free-text field that will be placed as a RAW (non-JSON-encoded) CLI argument value if it starts with `--`: specifically `decision` and `escalationId` in `validateResolveEscalationRequest`, `evidence` in `validateRecordHumanGateRequest`, and `feature`'s slug in `validateFeatureSlug` (all four flow into a raw argv position in `orky-action-dispatcher.ts`). A minimal, precise guard is `if (value.startsWith('--')) return { ok:false, error: '<field> must not start with "--" (would be misread as a CLI flag)' }` in each of those validators, or centralize it as a shared `rejectFlagLike(field, value)` helper reused by all of them. Add a regression test asserting `evidence:'--force'` and `decision:'--anything'` (and a `feature`/`escalationId` value starting with `--`) are rejected with `invalid-args` before any CLI is spawned. Longer-term, note in `docs/features/orky-action-dispatch.md` that any FUTURE raw (non-JSON) argv field added to this dispatcher must go through the same guard, since `execFile`'s argument-array safety (REQ-010) prevents SHELL injection but does not prevent the downstream CLI's own naive flag-scanning `parseArgs` from reinterpreting a value that looks like a flag — a distinct vulnerability class (CWE-88, argument injection) that the array-based invocation alone does not close.
